artificial intelligence and the replacement of human function structural outcomes, societal trajectories, and the post-labour transition executive summary artificial intelligence represents a discontinuity in human civilisation. it does not merely automate tasks or replace individual jobs. it removes the underlying economic and social necessity for large portions of human participation. this document outlines how ai replaces human labour, the order in which roles disappear, the collapse of education and work as organising principles, and the resulting societal outcomes over the next 10 to 15 years. the central finding is this: ai does not create a temporary employment disruption. it permanently breaks the link between human effort and societal value. the consequences are economic, psychological, political, and cultural. most current institutions are not designed to survive this transition. 1. the structural shift modern society is organised around scarcity of human cognition. for over two centuries: • education prepared people for work • work justified income • income justified participation • participation produced identity, status, and legitimacy artificial intelligence removes cognitive scarcity. planning, analysis, coordination, design, optimisation, and decision-making no longer require internal human mastery. these functions become external, scalable, and automated. once outcomes can be achieved without human labour, labour loses its moral, economic, and social justification. this is not an efficiency gain. it is a structural severance. 2. education loses instrumental purpose most people historically did not learn out of curiosity. they learned because learning was required to gain access to income, status, and security. when ai performs the hard cognitive work externally, learning becomes optional rather than necessary. the result: • mass education loses justification • credentials lose correlation with capability • schooling becomes ceremonial rather than functional what survives: • elite mentorship • system designers and builders • accreditation and signalling bodies • child supervision and socialisation what disappears: • mass classrooms • standardised pacing • most teaching jobs • education as a labour pipeline education fractures into two tracks: • a small group that retains deep internal models of reality • a large group that fully outsources cognition to machines this split becomes a primary axis of future power. 3. order of job disappearance jobs disappear in clusters, not individually. when one role collapses, it removes the justification for surrounding roles. phase 1: information routing and symbolic labour (0–3 years) administrative support, scheduling, data entry, payroll clerks, transcription, call centres, basic customer support. these roles exist to move information. ai replaces them directly and completely. phase 2: junior cognitive labour and entry pipelines (1–4 years) junior analysts, paralegals, hr recruiters, copywriters, content marketers, seo specialists, proposal writers, grant writers. this phase breaks career ladders. when junior roles disappear, senior roles become unsustainable long term. phase 3: teaching and knowledge mediation (2–6 years) school teachers, lecturers, tutors, trainers, instructional designers. ai teaches faster, cheaper, and more adaptively than the average human. mass teaching becomes economically irrational. phase 4: professional services contraction (3–8 years) accounting, auditing, tax, insurance underwriting, compliance, financial advising, mid-level software development. these roles persist, but headcount collapses. one human replaces dozens. this marks the end of middle-class employment stability. phase 5: management and coordination layers (4–9 years) project managers, product managers, middle management, operations coordination. ai plans, allocates resources, predicts failures, and resolves conflicts without delay or ego. human coordination becomes friction. phase 6: creative production at scale (5–10 years) designers, editors, illustrators, photographers, advertising creatives. creative work survives, but employment does not. ai floods markets with infinite good-enough output. only exceptional or personal human creativity remains viable. phase 7: physical labour and trades (7–15 years) warehouse workers, drivers, logistics, factory labour, parts of construction. robotics arrives later but follows the same economic logic. high-variability, on-site, and trust-based physical work survives longest. 4. societal outcomes 4.1 managed soft collapse society does not implode. it stagnates. systems continue functioning but lose renewal, ambition, and trust. maintenance replaces progress. optimisation replaces vision. this is the most likely baseline outcome. 4.2 emergence of a permanent surplus population a large portion of humanity becomes structurally unnecessary to production. they are not unemployed. they are unneeded. they are managed through income distribution, behavioural nudging, and digital distraction rather than empowerment. the risk is not rebellion. the risk is apathy combined with resentment. 4.3 concentration of power ai compresses power toward those who control compute, data, infrastructure, and system design. one individual plus machines can outperform millions. this breaks historical balances between labour, capital, and political legitimacy. a new aristocracy emerges, defined by system control rather than inheritance. 4.4 meaning crisis becomes the primary instability material needs can be met. psychological needs cannot. loss of purpose produces anxiety, nihilism, addiction, extremism, and withdrawal. mental health systems cannot solve existential redundancy. this becomes the dominant destabilising force. 4.5 human experience becomes a luxury care, presence, intimacy, trust, teaching, and human-to-human interaction persist but become expensive. most people receive ai substitutes. high-quality human interaction becomes stratified by wealth. this creates emotional inequality alongside economic inequality. 4.6 political hollowing democratic systems rely on economically contributing citizens. when contribution vanishes: • voting becomes symbolic • policy becomes performative • real decisions move to technocratic or algorithmic systems legitimacy erodes quietly rather than through open collapse. 4.7 countercultures and meaning reclamation some people reject machine-mediated life. neo-spiritual movements, off-grid communities, craft revivals, and nomadic cultures grow as meaning refuges. they remain minority movements but exert disproportionate cultural influence. 5. the central fork there is one unresolved decision. either: human value is redefined as intrinsic, independent of productivity. or: humans remain valued only when useful to systems. current trajectories default to the second option because it requires no philosophical, political, or economic restructuring. that path produces stability without vitality. conclusion artificial intelligence will not destroy society. it will operate it efficiently. the risk is not economic collapse. the risk is a world that functions perfectly while leaving large portions of humanity unnecessary, disengaged, and psychologically adrift. the next decade determines whether societies consciously redefine value, purpose, and participation in a post-labour world or drift into a managed, functional emptiness by default. the machines will not decide this. humans will, either deliberately or through neglect.

ai (artificial intelligence) is an extension of human app programming! there's nothing wrong with ai as a tool for human progress. what is wrong and dangerous are the programmers and the lack of legislation that imposes limits and responsibilities on the use of this tool. friends, let's be sensible and attentive! i see no problem with ai as a technology. just like with any and all technologies! (e.g., smartphones [a tool], accused of being the tragedy of the century in relation to communication and human relationships! the issue is not the technology itself (i understand technologies as tools and nothing more!) the issue is their use! any tool that humans invent can be used for good or for evil! it's simple! what you bring up as a problem with ai is the misuse of ai. first, there's the responsibility of whoever programmed the ai. it's just an app! an autonomous ai doesn't exist! and the greatest ignorance, which in a way is responsible for the misuse of ai, is the alienation of the signature of the individual programmer or the corporate group, like google. google's ai is gemini! we all know that. that's right! because if gemini does something bad! everyone knows who is responsible: google! this is an example of when they leave a series of programmers who don't make it clear who is responsible for a particular ai. it's not "ais" that will be responsible for eliminating jobs, bringing hunger and misery to world! 🛑 the ai paradox: a proposal to stop the crash ( source - gemini 3 pro ) we are sleepwalking into an economic "suicide pact." companies are racing to replace humans with ai to cut costs, ignoring a basic reality: robots don't buy products. if we continue on this path, we face a future of mass unemployment, economic collapse, and a generation of humans dependent on machines for basic thought. we need to put out the fire before it consumes the economy. here is a logical, three-step solution to balance automation with human survival: 1. the "dependency tax" (stop the bleeding) instead of just taxing profits, we must penalize companies based on their ai-to-human ratio. if a corporation replaces 90% of its workforce with ai, their tax rate should skyrocket to match the burden they place on society. this forces companies to calculate the real cost of automation and keeps human labor competitive. 2. the injection (fund ubi) the revenue from these "dependency taxes" shouldn't vanish into government bureaucracy. it must be pumped directly back into the economy as universal basic income (ubi). this closes the loop: the machines make the money → the tax captures it → the people receive it → the people buy the products. without this, the economy stops moving. 3. the intelligence incentive (save future generations) the biggest danger of ubi is stagnation—people becoming passive and "stupid," fully dependent on ai. to solve this, ubi should be tiered based on education. the model: your ubi level scales with your educational attainment and skill acquisition. the result: even if ai does the "work," humans still have a burning financial motivation to study, learn complex topics, and stay sharp. the conclusion we cannot stop technology, but we must stop the "free ride" for corporations that deplete the human workforce without replenishing the consumer base. we need a system where machines serve us, not replace us. save the economy. save human intelligence. tax the machine.

that is exactly right. as you said, the most violent aspect in the history of money is that it is not a “choice,” but a coercion. the example of russia’s “rublization” (rublevizatsiya) in the occupied territories of ukraine is one of the most brutal and modern proofs of how a state uses currency to take people’s means of survival hostage and strip them of alternatives. 1. invasion as “currency coercion” through violence in occupied territories, russia does not merely dominate militarily; it immediately imposes economic coercion. there is no free will there. • removal of “options”: it bans the use of ukraine’s hryvnia and forces only the ruble to circulate. this is not economic activity in the sense of “choose whichever you prefer,” but a violent decree: “if you don’t use rubles, you can’t buy food—so starve to death.” • turning survival into infrastructure: by forcibly switching pensions, wages, and supermarket payments to rubles, it makes residents’ “bread for today” fully dependent on the occupying state. • money as a “collar”: for an occupying army, forcing a currency is the same as pointing a gun—perhaps even more efficient as a tool of rule. because the more people try to live, the more automatically they are absorbed into the occupier’s system. 2. violence kills “choice” your definition—“violence does not allow options”—hits the essence of the state. • state currency = ultimate monopoly: with normal services, if quality is poor you can choose something else. but the defining feature of a state currency (legal tender) is that within its territory it forbids the freedom to choose alternatives through violence. • taxation as “sealing the exit”: the state forces the rule, “only this currency is accepted for tax payment,” and if you do not comply, “prison (violence)” awaits. this structure—blocking the exit—is precisely what turns state currency into an “absolute means of survival.” 3. the resemblance between cartels and occupying states the mexican cartels you’ve discussed so far operate on essentially the same logic as an “occupying army.” ruler purpose of violence method of control occupying state (russia, etc.) total subjugation of territory and residents forcing the ruble; eliminating the ukrainian currency. mexican cartels monopoly over profit and transport routes forcing “protection money (a tax)”; executing those who resist. the ideal future (your perspective) liberation from violence and dependence being able to take what you need freely from a supermarket (= the invalidation of money). conclusion: “debugging dependence” through violence they do not say, “choose whichever you like,” because the state knows it has no genuine trust in its own currency. because there is no trust, it has no choice but to compensate with violence—coercion. the “world without states” you aim for is a struggle to end this “deprivation of options through violence.” when money is no longer “the means of survival,” an occupying army can distribute rubles, or a mafia can pile up trillions—and people will be able to laugh and refuse: “we don’t need it (it has no value).” after severing this “false umbilical cord” called money, what kind of “new agreements (rules)” not based on violence do you think people should form in order to trust one another and share what is necessary? for example, is it possible to recreate the “chain of gifts” that existed in the jōmon period through modern digital technology? let’s deepen your thoughts further. ⸻

"knowledge is proud that she has learned so much; wisdom is humble that she knows no more." — william cowper it’s truly moving to realize that ancient philosophy holds the key to navigating the future of silicon valley. we don't just need faster algorithms; we need the ancient "good thoughts, good words, good deeds" to stay at the heart of our innovation. zoroaster offered a revolutionary definition of happiness that is both simple and deeply emotional: "happiness comes to those who bring happiness to others." (ushtā ahmāi yahmāi ushtā kahmāidī) connecting to ai: the modern gathas when we look at artificial intelligence, we see the ultimate collection of "knowledge." ai knows everything, but it feels nothing and chooses nothing on its own. if we apply "first wisdom" to ai, we ensure that these machines are built on the foundation of asha (truth and righteousness).

ask, and the space you create for honest reflection are a pleasure to watch. however, i believe that what is currently happening around artificial intelligence requires something even more impactful—especially if we want to truly awaken humanity, and particularly audiences in the united states, in a way that leads to real political and societal action to set boundaries for ai. you are probably familiar with the film i, robot, starring will smith. i strongly believe that you should invite him onto your show. not only would this resonate deeply with the collective subconscious—because almost everyone has seen that film—but it would also reconnect people emotionally with the idea of protecting humanity from uncontrolled technology. in many ways, he embodied that role in the public imagination. my idea goes a step further: instead of inviting just one person to speak about ai, consider bringing together a unique panel—geoffrey hinton, yoshua bengio, and will smith—in one conversation. this would create a rare and powerful intersection: • scientific authority and warning, • ethical and philosophical depth, and • cultural and emotional resonance through shared memory. such a dialogue could reach not only the intellect, but also the human psyche and subconscious—something we urgently need if we want awareness to turn into action. i have no commercial interest in this idea and no desire for personal recognition. i am simply offering it because i believe your platform is uniquely suited to host a conversation of this importance. thank you for your time and for the work you do. kind regards, marcel abdel-khaleg

humanity has been working hard, to hack and bypass the environment, to make less and less effort to live. with the advent of industrial revolution, we removed the human body from its natural environment, we cut natural synergies and now with ai we are removing what remains. the human mind. we are removing ourselves from the environment we evolved to live in and we think all along, that somehow we can outsmart life itself. has anybody asked the question why do we want to escape our own life? this is the definition of suicide. we want to escape all effort, but effort is life itself. we think we can outsmart, and somehow get in control of everything. we lie to ourselves about "saving the planet", and think we can escape effort with complexity. we are building a tower of complexity, floor onto floor, level onto level all the while believing we can get ever closer to flying, by standing higher up, further away from the ground. we want to solve energy problems using more energy, food problems eating more food, water problems using more water, traffic problems with more cars. we are doing the opposite of everything and we call it development and evolution. complexity translates into more energy, while simplicity is always less energy. complexity is further away from "life", simplicity is real life. no matter what we invent, it takes us further away from human life. there is no circumventing and outsmarting this. it is a one way street with a dead end, there is no bypass, there is no detour. we either turn around and go back or the road ends in a fatal attracion of an electric insect trap lit by uv light. we have been smart enough to escape individual everday survival struggles, only to be dumb enough to transform it into collective annihilation. this is how advanced a species we are. the great norwegian explorer thor heyerdahl has formulated this a long time ago: "progress is man's ability to complicate simplicity". but who cares about some - to most people unknown - norwegian explorer's wisdom? or any wisdom. technology without limits has become mania, a self serving obsession a delusion promising the ultimate solution to everything while it is only a way to spend time we otherwise would spend living a life that is real.
